channel.title: Cariboo Signals
channel.copyright: © 2026 Erich Zirnhelt. Licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0.
channel.itunes:subtitle: Technology and society in rural BC with Riley and Casey
channel.itunes:author: Erich Zirnhelt
channel.itunes:summary:
How do rural communities grow alongside technology? Daily conversations about responsible tech progress in the Cariboo region on the traditional territories of the Secwépemc, Tŝilhqot'in, and Dakelh nations. Riley brings tech systems thinking with rural roots, while Casey focuses on community development. New episodes every day with weekly themes.
Community Accountability: Cariboo Signals scores 18/20 (Exemplary) on the Community Content Compact — Track A (Community-Insider production). Transparency: 4/4 · Source Integrity: 3/3 · Displacement: 2/2 · Consent & Attribution: 3/4 · Benefit Flow: 4/5 · Accountability: 2/2. Produced by Erich Zirnhelt, Horsefly, BC (Secwépemc territory). Corrections and PSA opt-outs: feedback@cariboosignals.ca.
channel.description: How do rural communities grow alongside technology? Daily conversations about responsible tech progress in the Cariboo region on the traditional territories of the Secwépemc, Tŝilhqot'in, and Dakelh nations. Community Content Compact: 18/20 Exemplary (Track A). Self-funded, non-commercial. Producer: Erich Zirnhelt, Horsefly, BC. Corrections: feedback@cariboosignals.ca.

trace:assessment.trace:community: Rural Cariboo region, BC — Secwépemc, Tŝilhqot'in, and Dakelh territories

item.title: Arts, Culture And Digital Storytelling
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down Tod Maffin and [Williams Lake Tribune] ‘Escaped Alone’ wins best production in Williams Lake, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can rural communities maintain authentic cultural voices and documentation when the infrastructure—connectivity, platforms, and AI tools—is controlled by external corporations? Topics include: Platform-independent publishing and the Tod Maffin/Canadaland models, Rural broadband infrastructure monopolies and the Rogers-Shaw merger enforcement gap, Community-owned broadband alternatives and the Olds, Alberta fiber co-op model, Indigenous cultural documentation and governance through tools like Mukurtu, Funding structures for cultural organizations and the project-versus-infrastructure mismatch.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society

Sources discussed:

  • Newsletter: f***@mail.beehiiv.com: Tod Maffin - Official Web Site

item.title: Science, Wonder And The Natural World
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Science, Wonder & the Natural World.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Engadget] Parker Solar Probe makes another flyby of the sun, solar energy bags a win, and more science stories and [Business Insider] Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei warned Mythos posed a national security threat. Washington just responded., and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can rural communities like the Cariboo build sustainable, locally-valuable scientific and experiential infrastructure around their natural assets without depending on external funding cycles? Topics include: Dark sky preservation and International Dark-Sky Association designation process, Citizen science platforms (Globe at Night) and data governance, Space weather infrastructure gaps in rural communities, Ecotourism economics and sustainability models versus grant-cycle dependency, Indigenous knowledge systems and territorial relationships to the sky.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Salvation Army Williams Lake

item.title: Cariboo Local Affairs
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Cariboo Local Affairs.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] Williams Lake rejects jail closure idea, seeks government support on repeat crime and [Boing Boing] Man jailed after throwing ninja star at woman, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can a provincial Seniors Advocate's visit to rural Cariboo communities meaningfully improve policy outcomes, or will it remain performative without sustained follow-up and community preparation? Topics include: Role of the BC Seniors Advocate and measurable past policy outcomes, Capacity gaps in local media and their impact on accountability reporting, Organizational preparation required for rural communities to effectively engage with provincial officials, The broader civic infrastructure of rural communities (agricultural shows, cultural events, volunteer capacity), Structural design failures in how provincial government reaches rural populations.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: CMHA South Cariboo

item.title: Wild Spaces And Outdoor Life
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Al Jazeera English] Bolivian farmers clash with police as ex-president vows resistance and [Boing Boing] In 1932, John W. Campbell imagined machines outliving humanity, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can wildlife management frameworks keep pace with changing wild spaces, particularly regarding grizzly population recovery and human encroachment in the Chilcotin? Topics include: Yellowstone camping ban and grizzly population recovery as management case study, Jurisdictional gaps between provincial wildlife management and Tŝilhqot'in Aboriginal title in the Chilcotin, Remote monitoring technology capabilities and cost-benefit analysis, Sustainability challenges of grant-dependent monitoring infrastructure, Pre-trip stewardship communication protocols as governance model.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Family Enrichment Centre

item.title: Indigenous Lands And Innovation
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [BC Gov News] Opening new homes for Indigenous families in Prince Rupert and [Engadget] German court holds Google liable for false AI Overview answers, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Should Indigenous knowledge systems be integrated into existing technology and data infrastructure, or should the conceptual framework itself be fundamentally reimagined starting from Indigenous land-based governance? Topics include: Integration vs. foundational reimagining of Indigenous knowledge systems in technology, Data sovereignty, OCAP principles, and the limits of encoding relational knowledge, Institutional independence and funding relationships as constraints on accountability, Community-led infrastructure ownership versus service delivery models, Specific case studies: Animikii, FirstVoices, Tŝilhqot'in, Lhtako Dené Nation, and BCFNJC Police Accountability Unit.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Denisiqi Services Society

Sources discussed:

  • BC Gov News: [BC Gov News] Opening new homes for Indigenous families in P...
  • Lawrence Bonk (Engadget): [Engadget] German court holds Google liable for false AI Ove...
  • Newsletter: n***@animikii.com: Robots behaving badly VS what the land knows

item.title: Gear, Gadgets And Practical Tech
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Gear, Gadgets & Practical Tech.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Al Jazeera English] South Korean police break up 35-hour polling station protest and [Al Jazeera English] Ebola border shutdown leaves goods rotting between Uganda and DRC, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can rural communities like the Cariboo access reliable information about which gear and gadgets actually perform durably in remote, cold conditions without manufacturer support or institutional resources? Topics include: Battery chemistry performance in cold climates and manufacturer lock-in practices, The gap between institutional equipment specifications (SAR, wildfire) and what individual rural users can access, Formal vs. informal knowledge infrastructure and the discoverability problem, Structural sustainability of community databases and the handoff from student projects to permanent hosting, Municipal infrastructure as a persistence layer for community knowledge versus grant-dependent websites.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cedar Crest Society for Community Living

item.title: Working Lands And Industry
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Working Lands & Industry.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Business Insider] A consulting firm is now using AI to give free advice that once cost millions and [CBC Arts] Hasbro releases AI versions of its iconic characters, including Transformers, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Who captures the economic value generated by resource extraction and infrastructure development in the Cariboo-Chilcotin region, and how can communities ensure they retain benefits rather than bearing costs? Topics include: Carbon markets as mechanisms for pricing ecosystem services and their volatility risks, Ownership structure differences between Indigenous joint ventures and external contractor models, Data centre infrastructure costs to watersheds and power systems versus concentrated financial benefits, Asset ownership versus management contracts as pathways to permanent value capture, Technical and institutional capacity constraints preventing communities from negotiating favorable terms.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Chilcotin Rehabilitation Ltd.

item.title: Arts, Culture And Digital Storytelling
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Business Insider] Music festivals gave me hearing loss. My best friend and I started making earplugs, and it's now a multimillion-dollar business. and [Al Jazeera English] Kenyan graduates turn to AI tools for farming as jobs dry up, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: When platforms choose not to filter or label AI-generated content despite having the technical capability, who bears the cost and how can authentic regional cultural production survive? Topics include: Platform design choices around AI content filtering and their impact on authentic regional cultural visibility, Capital asymmetry between AI companies and regional arts organizations, Discovery algorithms as a mechanism that makes synthetic content more visible than authentic local storytelling, Cooperative digital infrastructure models as alternative to platform-dependent distribution, Indigenous cultural production and platform-independent knowledge systems as structural resistance to AI generation.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Station House Gallery

item.title: Science, Wonder And The Natural World
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Science, Wonder & the Natural World.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Business Insider] The AI industry's profit problem is getting bigger, says Goldman Sachs' equity research chief and [Business Insider] I flew on a startup's one-of-a-kind electric plane that could reshape air travel by 2027, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can AI tools reshape ecological science in the Cariboo in ways that serve community interests, or do they primarily amplify existing extractive research structures? Topics include: Infrastructure costs of AI and ecological impact of data centres, Indigenous data sovereignty and structural exclusion from peer-reviewed research systems, Edge-deployed vs. cloud-based AI models for environmental monitoring, Community-owned data licensing and fee-for-access ecological knowledge systems, Funding and sustainability models for long-term community AI governance infrastructure.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Community Futures Cariboo Chilcotin

item.title: Introducing The Show
item.description:
New here? Start with this one. Riley and Casey introduce themselves, explain how the show is actually put together, and walk you through what to expect each day of the week.

Meet the hosts: Riley (tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (community-first skeptic of tech promises) — both AI-generated, and upfront about it. Claude (Anthropic) writes the scripts, OpenAI TTS API (Nova · Echo) voices them day to day (Riley = Nova, Casey = Echo), with Azure Neural TTS (Ava · Andrew) run in parallel for quality comparisons, and the finished audio is mixed with music in Python (pydub). Cariboo Signals self-assesses at 18/20 ("Exemplary") on the Community Content Compact, a transparency framework for AI-made content.

What to expect, day by day:

  • Monday — Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling: Local arts scene, festivals, creative economy, media, and cultural tech
  • Tuesday — Working Lands & Industry: Cariboo forestry, ranching, mining, agriculture — tech in the resource economy
  • Wednesday — Gear, Gadgets & Practical Tech: Consumer devices, maker projects, homelab, practical tools — technology you can hold, build, or deploy at home or on the job
  • Thursday — Indigenous Lands & Innovation: Tŝilhqot'in, Secwépemc, Dakelh — language, governance, land stewardship, and tech
  • Friday — Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life: Chilcotin wilderness, backcountry recreation, conservation tech, wildfire and climate
  • Saturday — Cariboo Local Affairs: Williams Lake council, CRD, SD27, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola — civic decisions, community voices, and local development that shape everyday life in the Cariboo
  • Sunday — Science, Wonder & the Natural World: Scientific discovery, natural phenomena, ecology, and the curiosity behind how our world works — grounded in the landscapes and living systems of the Cariboo

New episodes land every day — explore the back catalog, and subscribe to get tomorrow's as soon as it's ready.

Credits
Theme Song: Cariboo Signals by Suno
Content Curation & Script: Claude (Anthropic)
TTS Voices: OpenAI TTS API (Nova · Echo) (with Azure Neural TTS (Ava · Andrew) for comparison)
Cover Art: ChatGPT (OpenAI)
Podcast Coordination: Erich Zirnhelt
© 2026 Erich Zirnhelt. Licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0.

item.title: Cariboo Local Affairs
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Cariboo Local Affairs.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [100 Mile Free Press] Stunting driver pulled over, ticketed near Lac La Hache and [Williams Lake Tribune] Openly transgender woman announces intent to run for Williams Lake City Council, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can Williams Lake convert multiple simultaneous civic initiatives (jail forum, chamber crime resolution, municipal election) into meaningful outcomes, or is it activity without momentum? Topics include: Williams Lake jail closure forum and provincial political authority, Chamber of Commerce crime resolution escalation strategy, 2026 municipal election and candidate specificity on public safety, Infrastructure maintenance and active transportation initiatives, Community expertise, resilience, and the limits of knowledge extraction without compensation.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Quesnel & District Hospice Palliative Care Association

item.title: Wild Spaces And Outdoor Life
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [TechRadar] 'Who wouldn't want a large-scale industrial polluter sitting in their backyard?': California residents ban data centers as public backlash grows and [Android Authority] Walmart’s Onn accidentally leaks its cheaper alternative to Google Nest Cam with Floodlight, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How should communities balance AI data center development promises against environmental and landscape protection, and who should have decision-making authority? Topics include: Grid capacity and water demand impacts of data center expansion, Overstated permanent employment claims in tech industry siting proposals, Tŝilhqot'in Aboriginal title as legal protection mechanism, Regulatory capacity gaps in BC industrial approvals, Landscape and cultural value as unformalized stakes in approvals processes.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Chilcotin Conservation Society

item.title: Indigenous Lands And Innovation
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] Grief, sobriety, music: Williams Lake native Petty Boy Osley’s journey and [Global News] Red Earth, Shoal Lake Cree Nation evacuees home as wildfire situation improves, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can Indigenous communities build genuine leadership and governance authority over technology systems rather than merely participating in systems designed by others? Topics include: Leadership vs. participation in Indigenous tech governance, Brain drain and retention mechanisms in capacity-building programs, Governance authority as prerequisite for sustainable Indigenous tech development, Data sovereignty and benefit-sharing frameworks for territorial knowledge, Procurement policy as tool for structural advantage and self-sustainability.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Denisiqi Services Society

item.title: Gear, Gadgets And Practical Tech
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Gear, Gadgets & Practical Tech.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Hackaday] Testing LFP Battery Failure Modes With Overcharging and [The Verge] You can print, slice, and engrave using this desktop crafting machine, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can communities regain control over technology systems designed to be opaque and dependent, and what role should individual versus collective ownership play in that reclamation? Topics include: Cyberdeck DIY movement as response to surveillance-by-design in consumer devices, Right-to-repair versus device sovereignty as different upstream/downstream problems, 3D-printed construction as labor-cost solution for rural housing crises, Digital sovereignty for First Nations and Indigenous data management, Institutional procurement policies and technology governance frameworks.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Literacy Quesnel Society

Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Gear, Gadgets & Practical Tech.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Hackaday] Testing LFP Battery Failure Modes With Overcharging and [The Verge] You can print, slice, and engrave using this desktop crafting machine, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can communities regain control over technology systems designed to be opaque and dependent, and what role should individual versus collective ownership play in that reclamation? Topics include: Cyberdeck DIY movement as response to surveillance-by-design in consumer devices, Right-to-repair versus device sovereignty as different upstream/downstream problems, 3D-printed construction as labor-cost solution for rural housing crises, Digital sovereignty for First Nations and Indigenous data management, Institutional procurement policies and technology governance frameworks.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Literacy Quesnel Society

item.title: Working Lands And Industry
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Working Lands & Industry.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Boing Boing] Harvard students cheer graduation speaker encouraging them to mercilessly destroy AI and [The Tyee] BC’s Family Doctor Crisis Gets Even Worse, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What are the fundamental operational and structural gaps preventing Cariboo agricultural producers from accessing modern tools, markets, and infrastructure—and which gaps are actually solvable? Topics include: Agricultural-grade weather data infrastructure and mesonet models, Processing capacity and cold-chain infrastructure constraints for direct-to-consumer/regional beef markets, Blockchain-based traceability systems and provenance verification for regional producers, First Nations agricultural enterprises as alternative capital and land-tenure models, Extension service capacity and co-operative proposal-development as institutional gaps.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

item.title: Science, Wonder And The Natural World
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Science, Wonder & the Natural World.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [ScienceAlert] Your Blood May Contain an Evolutionary Relic Older Than Animals Themselves and [ScienceDaily] Adorable tiny blue octopus found nearly 6,000 feet beneath the Galápagos, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What does it mean for ecological management and knowledge systems when living organisms demonstrate capabilities and strategies more sophisticated than our scientific models predict, and who benefits from the discovery? Topics include: Carnivorous plant catch-and-release strategy and evolutionary optimization, Sea cucumber cellular autonomy and tissue regeneration without central nervous system, Indigenous knowledge systems vs. Western scientific discovery frameworks, Research funding models and institutional incentives for long-term regional presence, Science communication infrastructure gaps in rural Cariboo region.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Loaves & Fishes Outreach

item.title: Cariboo Local Affairs
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Cariboo Local Affairs.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down the week's top tech developments, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can municipal enforcement tools ('drastic measures') effectively address prolific offending in small cities, or do solutions require provincial-level intervention? Topics include: Municipal jurisdiction limitations versus provincial control of criminal justice and service provision, Integrated response teams and evidence-based models (Medicine Hat, Foundry Cariboo Chilcotin, ACT teams), The accountability gap between what decision-makers hear and what they document/act on, Cumulative service contraction in small cities (airline loss, healthcare pressures, concurrent service gaps), The role of municipal politicians as translators of specific needs to senior government.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Quesnel Women's Resource Centre

Sources discussed:

  • My Cariboo Now: [My Cariboo Now] Williams Lake City Councillor calls for dra...

Closing Music: “Give Me Hope” by Modern Pitch — rock, pop, indie. Free music via Jamendo, licensed under Creative Commons.

item.title: Wild Spaces And Outdoor Life
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] Cariboo communities to host local government leadership workshops ahead of 2026 elections and [Williams Lake Tribune] Quesnel caught off guard by Pacific Coastal service suspension, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can general-purpose AI tools reliably identify wildlife and assist with land stewardship in underrepresented geographic regions like the Cariboo, or do they require regional validation and community-controlled governance frameworks? Topics include: Regional accuracy gaps in AI training datasets for underrepresented geographies, Distinction between purpose-built ecological tools vs. general-purpose AI extended into outdoor contexts, Indigenous data sovereignty and Tŝilhqot'in territorial knowledge governance, Community science contribution to ecological baselines (iNaturalist, trail building), Risk of treating AI outputs as certainty rather than hypothesis in safety-critical contexts like wildlife identification.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: First Journey Trails & Indigenous Youth Mountain Bike Program

item.title: Indigenous Lands And Innovation
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [CBC Kamloops] Sacred fire at Williams Lake First Nation marks 5 years since Tk'emlúps te Secwépemc announcement and [IndigiNews] In Kjipuktuk, Indigenous photojournalism fellows end week with emotion-filled ceremony, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What does it take for Indigenous communities to build and control their own technology infrastructure rather than relying on external vendors and advisory roles? Topics include: Data sovereignty infrastructure ownership vs. advisory roles, OCAP principles and the gap between endorsement and implementation, Indigenous AI governance: advisory circles and decision-making authority, Cultural knowledge encoding in technology and intellectual property limitations, Shared vs. individual community infrastructure models and accountability structures.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Quesnel Tillicum Society / Native Friendship Centre

item.itunes:summary:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [CBC Kamloops] Sacred fire at Williams Lake First Nation marks 5 years since Tk'emlúps te Secwépemc announcement and [IndigiNews] In Kjipuktuk, Indigenous photojournalism fellows end week with emotion-filled ceremony, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What does it take for Indigenous communities to build and control their own technology infrastructure rather than relying on external vendors and advisory roles? Topics include: Data sovereignty infrastructure ownership vs. advisory roles, OCAP principles and the gap between endorsement and implementation, Indigenous AI governance: advisory circles and decision-making authority, Cultural knowledge encoding in technology and intellectual property limitations, Shared vs. individual community infrastructure models and accountability structures.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Quesnel Tillicum Society / Native Friendship Centre

item.title: Working Lands And Industry
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Working Lands & Industry.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] ‘Will miss caring for you’: Clearwater losing 4 doctors, recruitment efforts underway and [TechCrunch] What ClickUp’s mass layoff tells us about the future of work, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can working land operations in the Cariboo maintain long-term viability and succession when land financialization and investor ownership create structural incentives to sell rather than continue farming? Topics include: Land financialization and investor ownership of agricultural operations, Succession planning gaps in working land operations, Alternative ownership models: collective/Indigenous land tenure versus individual ownership, Producer data sovereignty and precision agriculture as competitive differentiation, Financing structures and credit union products designed for agricultural succession rather than liquidation.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Mt. Timothy Ski Society

item.title: Arts, Culture And Digital Storytelling
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Engadget] Spotify is launching verification badges for podcasts to help listeners avoid AI slop and [The Verge] AI video is moving beyond clip slop, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can technical transparency standards like TRACE effectively govern AI-generated cultural content, or do they require deeper institutional and legislative frameworks to be meaningful? Topics include: TRACE standard components (Transparency, Responsibility, Accountability, Consent, Explainability), Difference between transparency disclosure and authorization consent in cultural contexts, Indigenous data sovereignty and tiered access requirements (Mukurtu systems, OCAP principles), Implementation gaps in standards adoption (auditing infrastructure, platform enforcement), Community-scale accountability versus formal regulatory frameworks.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society

item.title: Science, Wonder And The Natural World
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Science, Wonder & the Natural World.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [ScienceDaily] Quantum ghost imaging works using only sunlight in stunning new experiment and [ScienceDaily] Scientists found a hidden Alzheimer’s trigger and shut it down, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can biodiversity monitoring infrastructure be built in the Cariboo to detect ecological changes while protecting Indigenous data sovereignty and ensuring findings actually influence resource decisions? Topics include: Data sovereignty and Indigenous knowledge governance in monitoring systems, iNaturalist and citizen science as infrastructure for biodiversity observation, River dissolved oxygen decline and salmon habitat degradation in the Cariboo, Epistemic authority: what counts as evidence in resource management decisions, Multi-stakeholder convening as a pathway to integrated monitoring and decision-making.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: 100 Mile House Food Bank Society

item.title: Cariboo Local Affairs
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Cariboo Local Affairs.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] Williams Lake RCMP seek witnesses after assault on Borland Street and [Williams Lake Tribune] Planning underway for National Indigenous Peoples Day celebrations in Williams Lake, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: When Cariboo residents use political tools like petitions, conventions, and community organizing, do these efforts actually produce outcomes or merely create the appearance of participation? Topics include: The 100 Mile House ER petition and rural healthcare staffing challenges, NCLGA conventions as a rural advocacy mechanism versus individual petitions, Barriers to small volunteer community initiatives (Lebourdais Park movie night example), Political endorsements and leadership accountability to rural communities (Doerkson-Milobar endorsement), Digital connectivity as infrastructure for civic self-governance rather than just economic development.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Women's Contact Society

item.title: Wild Spaces And Outdoor Life
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [WIRED] California’s Wildfire Season Is Already Overactive and [BC Gov News] Inviting public to comment on Sea to Sky land use objectives, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Should post-fire landscapes be managed back to their prior state, or monitored as dynamic systems in transition, and what role should technology play in that process? Topics include: Post-fire landscape recovery and ecological monitoring strategies, Technology integration for wildlife tracking and public safety (GPS collars, eDNA, trail apps), Data governance, liability, and sustainability of public-facing systems, First Nations data sovereignty and the Tŝilhqot'in Yalhiyalh program, Regional land use planning processes and recreational pressure on conservation areas.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: First Journey Trails & Indigenous Youth Mountain Bike Program

item.title: Indigenous Lands And Innovation
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down Technology shouldn't sacrifice the integrity of knowledge and Seattle University School of Law Hosts Conference on Indigenous Perspectives on AI, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: When technology enables discovery of concealed evidence (like residential school graves), how should communities balance the capacity to surface knowledge with governance frameworks that protect how that knowledge is used and disclosed? Topics include: Data sovereignty and community consent in residential school investigations, Technology capabilities (GPR, LiDAR) vs. governance frameworks for knowledge use, Federal funding architecture and its role as both enabler and gatekeeper, Academic research partnerships and data storage/publication control, AI risk to Indigenous knowledge in publicly accessible datasets.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Chilcotin Aboriginal Training Employment Centre

item.title: Gear, Gadgets And Practical Tech
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Gear, Gadgets & Practical Tech.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Hackaday] Using a Nintendo Switch to Speed Up a 3D Printer and [WIRED] Oto Smart Sprinkler Review (2026): Solar-Powered and Simple to Use, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: In geographically isolated rural regions like the Cariboo, what combination of technology, maintenance practices, and community infrastructure actually enables sustainable self-sufficiency and emergency preparedness? Topics include: Emergency preparedness technology: single-use vs. maintained rechargeable devices and their real-world reliability in rural heat conditions, 3D printing, ESP32 microcontrollers, and open-source fabrication as accessibility tools with real skill and infrastructure barriers, Proprietary vs. open-source firmware lock-in (Bambu Lab case study) and its disproportionate impact on geographically isolated small operators, Institutional sustainability of technical skill-building programs and knowledge transfer in communities with high population turnover, Data sovereignty and community control over fabrication tools in Indigenous and rural contexts.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Boys and Girls Club of Williams Lake

item.title: Working Lands And Industry
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Working Lands & Industry.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [ScienceDaily] AI reveals the invisible magnetic chaos wasting energy inside electric motors and [Tom's Hardware] Texas county passes data center ban for rural areas for a year, move comes in wake of AI data centers moving to remote areas to skirt regulations — state senator says counties cannot legally impose these bans, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Does the Cariboo have the institutional capacity to adopt agricultural innovations and manage competing land uses, or is the regional governance infrastructure too thin to implement solutions regardless of technological readiness? Topics include: Perennial grain economics and the yield-penalty transition problem in Cariboo agriculture, Agricultural financing gaps and why lender structures prevent innovation adoption, Regional research capacity deficits and the mismatch between global research agendas and local agricultural needs, Data centre land use competition with agriculture and grid reliability impacts, Indigenous-led land stewardship as an institutional capacity model, The timing mismatch between technological readiness, institution-building, and external land use pressures.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Chilcotin Coast Tourism Association

item.title: Arts, Culture And Digital Storytelling
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [TechCrunch] Wirestock raises $23M to supply creative multi-modal data to AI labs and [Open Culture] When the Nobel Prize Committee Rejected The Lord of the Rings: Tolkien “Has Not Measured Up to Storytelling of the Highest Quality” (1961), plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: When, if ever, is AI-generated cultural content justified, and what governance structures and guardrails should protect communities from extraction while enabling legitimate cultural participation? Topics include: NPR study on arts engagement and biological aging at cellular level, AI content flooding discoverability landscapes and what it displaces versus what it doesn't, Indigenous sovereignty and community control over cultural production tools and governance, Project-based funding gaps and the failure of pilots to become sustainable infrastructure, Extraction models in tech (Wirestock, IP-as-infrastructure) versus community-serving design.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Chilcotin Partners for Literacy

item.itunes:summary:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [TechCrunch] Wirestock raises $23M to supply creative multi-modal data to AI labs and [Open Culture] When the Nobel Prize Committee Rejected The Lord of the Rings: Tolkien “Has Not Measured Up to Storytelling of the Highest Quality” (1961), plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: When, if ever, is AI-generated cultural content justified, and what governance structures and guardrails should protect communities from extraction while enabling legitimate cultural participation? Topics include: NPR study on arts engagement and biological aging at cellular level, AI content flooding discoverability landscapes and what it displaces versus what it doesn't, Indigenous sovereignty and community control over cultural production tools and governance, Project-based funding gaps and the failure of pilots to become sustainable infrastructure, Extraction models in tech (Wirestock, IP-as-infrastructure) versus community-serving design.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Chilcotin Partners for Literacy

item.title: Science, Wonder And The Natural World
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Science, Wonder & the Natural World.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [ScienceDaily] Stunning 150-million-year-old stegosaur skull rewrites dinosaur evolution and [ScienceDaily] Paleontology rocked by discovery of organic molecules in 66-million-year-old dinosaur bones, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can the Cariboo region develop locally-controlled, long-term scientific monitoring infrastructure using accessible methodologies without reproducing extractive research models? Topics include: Environmental DNA methodology for monitoring aquatic species without capture or disturbance, Indigenous data sovereignty and governance requirements for research conducted on traditional territories, Acoustic ecology and plant responses to environmental sound in forest regeneration contexts, Old-growth forests as scientific archives containing climate and cosmic event records, Community foundations and endowment models as sustainable funding vehicles for long-term ecological monitoring.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Quesnel Community Foundation

item.title: Cariboo Local Affairs
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Cariboo Local Affairs.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [100 Mile Free Press] HANSEN: Lac La Hache Community Club president steps up to run bingo and [Williams Lake Tribune] 2025 Williams Lake Fire Department recruits earn Live Fire Interior Attack certification, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What combination of external recognition, cultural transmission, and civic investment creates sustainable rural community resilience, and how do communities choose what to anchor their identity to? Topics include: The biomass award as external validation of local problem-solving, Cultural transmission through youth fiddle society and its relationship to retention, Leadership succession and bench depth in volunteer organizations, High-infrastructure anchors (junior hockey) versus low-infrastructure sustainable activities, The distinction between sustaining activities and anchoring community identity.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Williams Lake Women's Centre

item.title: Wild Spaces And Outdoor Life
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Resilience.org] Environmental protection depends on more than regulation and [Williams Lake Tribune] Help B.C. communities recover from wildfires 1 beer at a time, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can technology tools and targeted institutional interventions meaningfully improve wildfire preparedness and emergency survival readiness in rural Cariboo communities? Topics include: Satellite communicator technology accessibility and pricing for rural populations, AI-assisted emergency planning tools and demographic limitations, Interoperability and standards gaps in emergency communication devices, Community social infrastructure versus top-down institutional programs, Role of local institutions (credit unions, search and rescue, Regional Districts) in building preparedness.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

item.title: Indigenous Lands And Innovation
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [The Tyee] BC’s New Chief Treaty Commissioner on the Path Forward and [Global News] Manitoba’s nursing regulator apologizes for discrimination against Indigenous Peoples, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can Indigenous communities build internal technical capacity to implement data sovereignty frameworks, and what is the role of long-term human capital development versus short-term institutional solutions? Topics include: Indspire scholarship pipeline and long-term capacity building, UVic digital collections project and governance authority over digitization, FirstVoices as model for community-controlled digital infrastructure, Institutional hiring practices and the difference between advisory roles and decision-making authority, Funding models and structural durability: government dependency versus charitable sector diversification.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Williams Lake Indian Band

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can Indigenous communities build internal technical capacity to implement data sovereignty frameworks, and what is the role of long-term human capital development versus short-term institutional solutions? Topics include: Indspire scholarship pipeline and long-term capacity building, UVic digital collections project and governance authority over digitization, FirstVoices as model for community-controlled digital infrastructure, Institutional hiring practices and the difference between advisory roles and decision-making authority, Funding models and structural durability: government dependency versus charitable sector diversification.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

item.title: Gear, Gadgets And Practical Tech
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Gear, Gadgets & Practical Tech.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Tom's Hardware] Louis Rossmann tells 3D printer maker Bambu Lab to ‘Go (Bleep) yourself’ over its threatened lawsuit against enthusiast — Right to Repair advocate offers to pay the legal fees for a threatened OrcaSlicer developer and [Hackaday] Trying to Fix a GoPro Hero 10 With No Camera Input Issue, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can rural communities build sustainable, maintainable technology infrastructure when manufacturers increasingly restrict ownership rights and technical expertise is geographically concentrated? Topics include: Bambu Lab lawsuit and proprietary ecosystem lock-in versus right-to-repair, Hybrid DIY/commercial manufacturing models for complex systems, Rural technology equity and geographic access barriers, Federal and provincial right-to-repair legislation landscape, Tool-lending libraries and institutional infrastructure for rural tech access.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

item.title: Working Lands And Industry
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Working Lands & Industry.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [TechRadar] Fuelled by AI, Micron's $50 billion chip factory in Idaho will use billions of litres of water every year — but the US tech giant stays silent on where that new water will come from and [TechRadar] OpenAI's ‘Trusted Contact’ feature for ChatGPT users in crisis is a sign AI is becoming something much more personal, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can BC deploy AI-driven mineral exploration technology responsibly, or must governance capacity be built before the technology accelerates discovery? Topics include: AI mineral exploration technology and KoBold Metals' competitive advantages, Institutional capacity gaps in provincial and Indigenous consultation and assessment processes, Data sovereignty and public value capture from publicly-funded geological data, Nation-level technical capacity building as a structural intervention in resource governance, Sequencing of technology deployment versus governance system readiness.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Williams Lake Food Policy Council

item.title: Arts, Culture And Digital Storytelling
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [NYT Business] James Murdoch’s Company Said to Be in Talks to Acquire Major Parts of Vox Media and [Engadget] Spotify now lets AI agents like OpenClaw generate personal podcasts, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can rural cultural institutions build sustainable, discoverable storytelling capacity in an environment flooded with AI-generated content while maintaining community control and authentic relationships? Topics include: AI-generated content's impact on discoverability for authentic rural cultural storytelling, Governance models balancing community control with visibility (Mukurtu tiered-access system), Funding models for digital capacity at small cultural institutions (provincial arts funding vs. co-op models), Technology's relationship to community vitality (downstream not upstream), Place-specific authenticity as defense against synthetic content and media pressure.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Museum of the Cariboo Chilcotin

item.title: Science, Wonder And The Natural World
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Science, Wonder & the Natural World.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [NYT Top Stories] New Study Shows Risks of Amazon Deforestation. And Rewards of Protection. and [ScienceDaily] Scientists make stunning discovery that could change our understanding of the Universe, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can recent scientific discoveries about conserved mechanisms in regeneration, evolution, and quantum systems translate into actionable improvements for rural communities like the Cariboo? Topics include: Gene therapy and regenerative medicine accessibility in rural communities, Evolutionary conservation of genetic expression mechanisms and ecological succession prediction, Indigenous data sovereignty and co-investigator research partnerships with universities, Monitoring commons governance and the role of temporal sequence in system dynamics, Analytical capacity as the primary bottleneck rather than data availability or discovery.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Habitat for Humanity Cariboo

item.title: Cariboo Local Affairs
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Cariboo Local Affairs.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] New exhibitions at Williams Lake Station House Gallery celebrate B.C.’s wildlife, natural beauty and [Williams Lake Tribune] Walk held from Williams Lake to Tl’esqox First Nation for Sabrina Rosette, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What actually holds a rural community together, and how can external programs and policymakers effectively support the social and institutional infrastructure that enables this resilience? Topics include: The role of unglamorous infrastructure maintenance decisions in rural community resilience, Institutional leadership continuity and its non-transferable knowledge in rural development bodies, Low-barrier integrated youth mental health service models and funding sustainability, Indigenous-led economic development and cultural tourism as alternative rural capacity-building, The gap between program funding and workforce retention capacity in rural service delivery.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

item.title: Wild Spaces And Outdoor Life
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [BC Gov News] Day-use passes available for three popular parks, seasonal closures at Pipi7íyekw/Joffre Lakes Park and [The Tyee] Diary of an Accidental Birder, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Does BC's new lightning detection technology funding represent a meaningful advance in wildfire prevention, or does it risk becoming a demonstration project without the governance structure and procurement commitment to achieve real operational impact? Topics include: Lightning detection technology and its role in reducing detection-to-initial-attack timeframes, Fuel mapping data currency and its impact on ignition probability modeling accuracy, Drone-based thermal surveillance capabilities and Transport Canada BVLOS regulatory constraints, Indigenous Nation governance and territorial protocols in fire detection system deployment, Procurement strategy versus grant funding as mechanisms for technology sector viability and operational scaling.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Scout Island Nature Centre

item.title: Indigenous Lands And Innovation
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [The Tyee] BC’s New Chief Treaty Commissioner on the Path Forward and [BC Gov News] Minister’s statement on appointment of provincial commissioner to BC Treaty Commission, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can Indigenous knowledge be integrated into AI wildfire management systems equitably, or does the current technical and governance infrastructure inevitably replicate historical extraction patterns? Topics include: Data sovereignty versus Western privacy frameworks: relational vs. individual consent models, Technical irreversibility of abstracted knowledge in AI training and attribution/revocation problems, Capacity and scaling barriers: dependency on external firms versus long-term community workforce development, Funding architecture as extraction mechanism when communities have no governance stake or outcome definition authority, Procurement timing as leverage point: design-stage governance versus post-contract retrofit.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Carrier Chilcotin Aboriginal Training & Employment Centre

item.title: Gear, Gadgets And Practical Tech
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Gear, Gadgets & Practical Tech.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Hackaday] Sunlight Powered, Sunlight Readable: Solar Case for Nook Simple Touch and [Toms Guide] I got tired of waiting for a touchscreen iMac so I made one myself — here’s how I did it, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can remote Cariboo communities effectively bridge the gap between available technology and practical knowledge to solve real infrastructure and sustainability problems? Topics include: Off-grid solar power systems: specifications, realistic performance, and marketing vs. reality, Maker culture and open-source hardware documentation for practical problem-solving, Right-to-repair and device repairability as infrastructure equity issues, Repair cafés and community technical capacity-building models, First Nations technical knowledge and local innovation ecosystems.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

item.title: Working Lands And Industry
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Working Lands & Industry.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] Cariboo Chilcotin emergency management collective host emergency preparedness expo and [IndigiNews] In ‘B.C.’s’ interior, a syilx program is returning burrowing owls to the grasslands, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can diversification strategies like agrivoltaics and precision agriculture help small-to-medium working land operators survive margin compression, and what infrastructure investments would actually enable Cariboo producers to access these opportunities? Topics include: Agrivoltaics (solar + grazing) feasibility and ALR regulatory constraints in BC versus US models, Crown lease tenure as structural constraint on dual-land-use revenue diversification, Public-private partnerships in agricultural research and commercialization (Summerland cherry breeding case), Regional disparities in agricultural research investment and the case for Cariboo-specific breeding/extension capacity, Data sovereignty and producer-owned cooperative models for aggregating farm production data.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Williams Lake Stampede Association

item.title: Arts, Culture And Digital Storytelling
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [The Tyee] David Beers Wins the 2026 Digital Publishing Leadership Award and [NYT Top Stories] Mariachi Brothers Detained by ICE Invited to Open for Kacey Musgraves After Release, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can independent cultural production in rural regions like the Cariboo achieve financial sustainability without relying on precarious grant cycles? Topics include: Economic models for independent creative sustainability (recurring revenue, direct audience relationships, co-op structures), Grant landscape and funding mechanisms (Local Journalism Initiative, BC Arts Council, pilot-to-permanent transition challenges), Infrastructure constraints (cold-start problem, institutional anchors, community radio viability), Indigenous sovereignty and cultural production governance (co-governance requirements, funding disparity, direct resourcing to nations), Editorial independence in small communities (social pressure, accountability dynamics, coverage gaps).

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Station House Gallery

item.title: Science, Wonder And The Natural World
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Science, Wonder & the Natural World.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Boing Boing] Researchers built a language model that thinks it's still 1930 and [Business Insider] A couple bought a 380-square-foot shed for $12,000 and turned it into a tiny home they share with their 3 dogs, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can systematic ecological observation and community science build both scientific resilience and territorial sovereignty in the Cariboo region? Topics include: Citizen science platforms and distributed observation infrastructure, Data sovereignty and Indigenous-governed biodiversity data, Multi-stakeholder funding models for sustained environmental monitoring, The role of wonder versus obligation in driving long-term ecological observation, Community Futures' potential role as a regional convener.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

item.title: Cariboo Voices And Local News
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Cariboo Voices & Local News.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] Scheduled criminal trials at 11 B.C. courthouses may not be able to proceed and [Williams Lake Tribune] B.C. forests minister unveils new federal grant, says aid needs could reach $6B, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What institutional capacity do rural Cariboo communities actually need to sustain and maintain public services, and who bears responsibility for providing it? Topics include: Provincial playground investment and hidden operational maintenance costs, Rural community fraud vulnerability and financial institution design choices, Wildfire prevention infrastructure and Indigenous-led Guardian programs, Institutional capacity gaps in small municipalities and First Nations organizations, Local journalism's role in connecting disparate stories into structural pattern analysis.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Memorial Hospital Foundation

item.title: Wild Spaces And Outdoor Life
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Business Insider] The LifeStraw water filter deserves a place in any emergency kit and [Al Jazeera English] Rains revive Iraq’s wetlands after years of drought, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Deep dive on Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life Topics include: connectivity, data sovereignty, AI, infrastructure, funding.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

item.title: Indigenous Lands And Innovation
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down Rethinking Privacy Through Indigenous Data Sovereignty and Rethinking Privacy Through Indigenous Data Sovereignty, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Whose sovereignty does Canada's 'sovereign AI' plan actually serve, and does it advance or undermine Indigenous data sovereignty? Topics include: Distinction between national sovereignty and Indigenous data sovereignty in AI strategy, OCAP principles (Ownership, Control, Access, Possession) and implementation gaps, Data governance for relational Indigenous knowledge versus Western individual-privacy frameworks, AI for climate and accountability structures versus technical optimization, Guardian programs and knowledge extraction without adequate resource allocation, Parallel versus integrated Indigenous AI infrastructure models.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Friendship Society

item.title: Community Tech And Governance
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Community Tech & Governance.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [BC Gov News] Cracking down on organized crime, gun violence and [BC Gov News] Dewdney Bridge replacement reaches completion, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: When rural communities adopt external technology solutions (commercial or government-funded), what governance structures must exist to ensure sustainable, community-controlled outcomes? Topics include: Commercial vs. community-owned infrastructure as a false binary disguising the real variable of governance capacity, Indigenous language technology sustainability: funding, technical maintenance, and data sovereignty in federally-funded projects like CILT, Regional coordination models for Indigenous technical services and the risk of pooled decision-making erasing smaller communities' urgencies, Operations budget and embedded technical training as necessary conditions for technology adoption in resource-constrained communities, Governance readiness standards for rural digital infrastructure decisions across municipalities and First Nations.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Williams Lake Association for Community Living

item.title: Working Lands And Industry
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Working Lands & Industry.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Quartz] Boeing is losing less money as aircraft deliveries climb and its turnaround picks up speed and [The Tyee] Salmon Scientists Warn the DFO Is Killing a Crucial Lab, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Is the lack of working-landscape-appropriate equipment design and affordability a significant constraint on rural land stewardship in the Cariboo, or is it secondary to organizational capacity and governance issues? Topics include: Working-landscape vs. recreational equipment design differences and the customer-base drift problem, Durability, repairability, and cost structures appropriate for thin-margin operations vs. discretionary adventure budgets, Shared-asset and cooperative equipment ownership models scaled to regional or Nation level, Right-to-repair and manufacturer service dependency as operational constraints in remote geographies, Accumulated field knowledge and practice adaptation embedded in existing equipment.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Chilcotin Rehabilitation Ltd.

item.title: Arts, Culture And Digital Storytelling
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [The Marginalian] The Force and the Flower: Poet and Potter M.C. Richards on Creativity and [STAT News] Opinion: The podcast telling the stories behind Ambien, Ozempic, EpiPens, and other game-changing drugs, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How should Canada regulate digital platforms to protect rural and Indigenous cultural storytelling in the absence of platform accountability structures? Topics include: Platform algorithms as non-neutral distribution infrastructure that shapes what gets created, Data portability and creator sovereignty as components of duty of care frameworks, Community-owned alternative infrastructure (newsletters, podcasts, local archives) as durable alternatives to platform dependency, AI's role in cultural production: generation vs. connective discovery for community storytellers, Risks of overregulation creating automated content moderation harms to marginalized creators.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society

  • The Marginalian: [The Marginalian] The Force and the Flower: Poet and Potter ...

  • TechCrunch: [TechCrunch] Shade lands $14M to let creative teams search t...

item.itunes:summary:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [The Marginalian] The Force and the Flower: Poet and Potter M.C. Richards on Creativity and [STAT News] Opinion: The podcast telling the stories behind Ambien, Ozempic, EpiPens, and other game-changing drugs, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How should Canada regulate digital platforms to protect rural and Indigenous cultural storytelling in the absence of platform accountability structures? Topics include: Platform algorithms as non-neutral distribution infrastructure that shapes what gets created, Data portability and creator sovereignty as components of duty of care frameworks, Community-owned alternative infrastructure (newsletters, podcasts, local archives) as durable alternatives to platform dependency, AI's role in cultural production: generation vs. connective discovery for community storytellers, Risks of overregulation creating automated content moderation harms to marginalized creators.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society

item.title: Science, Wonder And The Natural World
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Science, Wonder & the Natural World.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Kottke.org] Trials for a pancreatic cancer mRNA vaccine :... and [Boing Boing] A 1946 plan to feed a billion people with sawdust, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can rural communities like the Cariboo be positioned to conduct long-duration, place-based observational science better than centralized institutions, and what governance structures would make this work? Topics include: Long-duration observational science as a structural advantage for rural communities, Indigenous data sovereignty and OCAP principles in shared monitoring infrastructure, Governance architecture and dispute resolution in data-sharing arrangements across competing land interests, Community Futures as a potential convening body for multi-stakeholder monitoring commons, Operational vs. scientific framing as the foundation for durable rural science initiatives.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Salvation Army Williams Lake

item.title: Cariboo Voices And Local News
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Cariboo Voices & Local News.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] TRAILBLAZER CHRONICLES: What different trails teach us about community and [Williams Lake Tribune] 5-year jail sentence for woman who killed partner near Quesnel, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What actually holds geographically dispersed, economically diverse communities like the Cariboo together functionally, and how fragile is that social infrastructure? Topics include: The role of events (Stampede, Food Fair) as social capital generators and community cohesion mechanisms, Volunteer infrastructure fragility and succession planning in rural communities, Healthcare volunteerism as both essential care and symptom of underfunding, Social capital theory: bonding versus bridging across community groups, Structural constraints on rural volunteering and hybrid funding models for non-profits.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Williams Lake Hospice Society

item.title: Wild Spaces And Outdoor Life
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [CFJC Today Kamloops] Small wildfire near Kamloops quickly classified as being held and [Global News] Nova Scotia’s provincewide ban on entering the woods was unreasonable, court rules, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can technology meaningfully shorten the gap between wildfire detection and community response, and what institutional changes are needed to make that technology operationally useful? Topics include: Wildfire detection technology vs. response infrastructure gaps, Outdated fuel type mapping and its impact on fire behavior prediction, The role of predictive services units in translating remote sensing data to operational use, Nation-operated fuel monitoring networks as complement to provincial capacity, Funding and institutional sustainability models for Indigenous-led land monitoring programs, Data governance and integration challenges in provincial wildfire management.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: First Journey Trails & Indigenous Youth Mountain Bike Program

item.title: Indigenous Lands And Innovation
item.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [APTN News] The next test for economic reconciliation is explored at Canada 2020 conference and [APTN News] Some people with medical needs are being taken out of Peguis First Nation, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can Indigenous communities deploy their territorial knowledge to build sovereignty rather than create vulnerability? Topics include: Institutional embeddedness vs. co-optation risk in Indigenous-state partnerships, Embodied knowledge transmission through cultural forms as resistance to data extraction, Technology platform ownership and long-term jurisdictional control, Capacity gaps between well-resourced and underfunded Indigenous nations, Digital security and surveillance as extensions of territorial knowledge vulnerabilities.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Denisiqi Services Society

item.itunes:summary:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [APTN News] The next test for economic reconciliation is explored at Canada 2020 conference and [APTN News] Some people with medical needs are being taken out of Peguis First Nation, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can Indigenous communities deploy their territorial knowledge to build sovereignty rather than create vulnerability? Topics include: Institutional embeddedness vs. co-optation risk in Indigenous-state partnerships, Embodied knowledge transmission through cultural forms as resistance to data extraction, Technology platform ownership and long-term jurisdictional control, Capacity gaps between well-resourced and underfunded Indigenous nations, Digital security and surveillance as extensions of territorial knowledge vulnerabilities.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).
